title: Dissipative generation of spin squeezing in the resolved vacuum Rabi splitting limit
authors: Edwin Chaparro, Eric Yilun Song, Diego Barberena, James K. Thompson, Ana Maria Rey, Jeremy T. Young
abstract: Harnessing dissipation in the presence of strong symmetries has recently emerged as a promising route for generating entanglement in atomic clocks. However, previous proposals relied on regimes where cavity photons can be adiabatically eliminated, significantly limiting their applicability to experimentally relevant cavity-QED regimes that lie in or near the resolved vacuum Rabi splitting regime. Here we show that symmetry-protected dissipative spin squeezing can be realized even when cavity photons actively participate in the dynamics, extending the experimental relevance of the protocol. We study a three-level ensemble of $^{87}\mathrm{Sr}$ atoms coupled to an optical cavity in the resolved vacuum Rabi splitting regime and demonstrate that, with smooth ramps of the drive amplitude and detunings, the driven-dissipative dynamics enters a stable low-photon regime in which nonadiabatic cavity excitations and sector-resolving photon leakage can be controlled. Within this low-photon operating window, sector-resolving photon leakage is suppressed and the sector-dependent geometric phase realizes effective one-axis twisting. At the end of the protocol the entanglement can also be efficiently transferred directly onto the long-lived clock states by turning the drive off. For experimentally realistic parameters, we theoretically show that more than $25\,\mathrm{dB}$ of squeezing can be generated for $10^5$ atoms, closely saturating the ideal one-axis twisting scaling $ξ_{\min}^2 \propto N^{-2/3}$. At fixed cooperativity, the optimized squeezing remains broadly comparable to the unresolved-regime implementation, while the resolved-regime implementation reaches comparable squeezing on a substantially shorter physical timescale. These results establish symmetry-protected dissipative dynamics as a practical route to beyond the standard-quantum-limit performance in optical-clock platforms.
summary: This paper presents a method to generate high levels of spin squeezing in atomic clocks by utilizing dissipative dynamics in the resolved vacuum Rabi splitting regime. By carefully controlling the system through a multi-stage protocol, the authors show that the cavity photons actively participate in the process without causing decoherence. This establishes a practical and experimentally relevant route to surpass the standard quantum limit in optical clock platforms.
main_problem: The paper addresses how to generate spin squeezing in atomic clocks using dissipative dynamics, specifically extending protocols to the resolved vacuum Rabi splitting (RVRS) regime where cavity photons cannot be adiabatically eliminated.
main_result: They demonstrate that symmetry-protected dissipative spin squeezing is achievable in the RVRS regime, achieving theoretical squeezing levels exceeding 25 dB for $10^5$ atoms on a shorter timescale than previously thought possible.
method: The analysis utilizes the Lindblad master equation, employing techniques like the Dissipative Truncated-Wigner Approximation (DissTWA) and analyzing the accumulation of geometric (Berry) phases.
model_or_system: The system is a three-level ensemble of $^{87}\mathrm{Sr}$ atoms coupled to an optical cavity. The dynamics are governed by a master equation incorporating cavity decay and atomic interactions.
key_observables: Spin squeezing ($\xi_2$), geometric phase ($\Phi_B$), and cavity quadrature amplitudes ($\hat{x}, \hat{p}$).
important_parameters: Resolved Vacuum Rabi Splitting (RVRS) regime, low-photon operating window, and the ratio of cavity decay rate ($\kappa$) to vacuum Rabi splitting ($\Omega_{	ext{VRS}}$).
main_assumptions: The protocol relies on smooth ramps of drive amplitude and detunings to maintain a stable, low-photon operating window that suppresses nonadiabatic excitations and leakage.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison between the UVRS and RVRS regimes, showing the evolution of cavity quadratures and the three-stage protocol involving drive ramping, detuning, and turning off the drive.
paper_structure: The paper builds by first establishing the general framework for dissipative squeezing, then contrasting the solvable UVRS limit (where adiabatic elimination is valid) with the more complex RVRS limit, detailing a three-stage protocol to realize effective one-axis twisting.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it pushes the boundaries of quantum metrology by showing how to harness complex, non-adiabatic cavity dynamics (RVRS) for high-precision sensing, moving beyond simplified theoretical limits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Cavity-QED platform and vacuum Rabi regimes. (a) An ensemble of three-level atoms (ground |↓⟩, clock |↑⟩, optical |e⟩) couples collectively to a single-mode cavity at rate g. The input drive at frequency ωd and ampli- tude √καin populates the mode, which loses photons at rate κ (equivalently, has linewidth κ). We take ωc = ωa unless stated otherwise; atoms are initially prepared in a coherent ground–clock superposition while the cavity starts in vacuum. (b)–(c) The hybridization of the collective atomic excitation with the cavity photon produces polaritons split by the low- excitation vacuum Rabi frequency (ΩVRS = 2g√NJ). In the unresolved vacuum Rabi splitting (UVRS) limit (left),...

FIG. 2. Multistage protocol for generating and stor- ing spin squeezing. The top panel shows a schematic of the time-dependent profiles of the detuning (blue dashed line) and the drive amplitude (solid red line). The panel below de- scribes the protocol. Stages I–III are displayed in separate columns; the top rows show the Bloch sphere of the driven manifold {|↓⟩, |e⟩} with collective spin ˆJ, the middle row the Bloch sphere of the encoded spin ˆS spanned by |0⟩≡|↑⟩and |1⟩≡α |↓⟩+β |e⟩, and the bottom row has a phase-space rep- resentation of the cavity mode ˆa. In Stage I, a resonant drive rotates ˆJ into a spin-polarized steady state, |1⟩, while the cav- ity relaxes to the vacuum state. In...

FIG. 3. Dynamics of the cavity-mode quadratures ˆx and ˆp. The solid lines show the full cavity quadratures, while the dashed lines show the instantaneous adiabatic-elimination predictions ˆxAE and ˆpAE from Eq. (28). The shaded regions indicate the stages of the protocol. All curves are computed for N = 105, δσ/(NΓ0) = ∆c/(NΓ0) = 1.33 × 10−3, and g = 10 kHz. In panel (a), corresponding to the UVRS regime with κ = 15g√NJ and √καin = 0.5gNJ/2, the cavity closely follows the adiabatic estimate. In panel (b), corresponding to the RVRS regime with κ = 15g, κ/(g√NJ) = 6.7×10−2 ≪1, and √καin = 0.487gNJ/2, the full quadratures exhibit visible oscillatory deviations from the adiabatic estimate.

FIG. 4. Mean-field phase diagram. Steady-state spin polarization ⟨ˆJz⟩ss/(NJ/2) as a function of scaled detuning ∆c/(NΓ0) and normalized drive αin/αref, for ωa = ωc. The reference amplitude αref is defined in Eq. (29). FIG. 5. Spin-squeezing dynamics. Here we compare the ramped protocol (blue) and a sudden quench (red) with the HP predictions (green). The background color indicates the three stages of the multistage protocol. The insets show the intracavity photon number ⟨ˆn⟩, which remains low for the ramped protocol but can increase transiently after sudden switching. In both panels N = 105 and αin/αref = 0.5. The time axis is reported in units of NΓ0t, with Γ0 evaluated using the panel-specific cavity linewidth κ. (a) UVRS: κ = 15g√NJ and ∆c = 2.5 × 10−2NΓ0.
